title: Dumpuary Is Dead. These Movies Killed It. Plus: The Best Movies at Sundance and the Catherine O’Hara Hall of Fame.
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins argue that a good January (led by Markiplier's self-financed Iron Lung) killed Dumpuary, mark the passing of Catherine O'Hara, run through Sean's Sundance haul, review the Netflix rom-com People We Meet on Vacation and Charli XCX's The Moment, then Sean talks with production designer Jack Fisk about Marty Supreme.

segments.heading: The news
segments.note: box office and a Nancy Meyers greenlight

films.title: Iron Lung
films.note: The headline story: Markiplier (Mark Fischbach) self-financed and self-distributed a $3M submarine horror adapted from the video game, willed it onto 4,100 screens by telling his 39M YouTube subscribers to call their theaters, and made ~$18M in three days. Sean didn't think much of the movie itself (quiet, repetitive, good sound design, a man in a mini-sub crossing a sea of blood), but calls the release one of the most fascinating things to happen to theatrical exhibition in a decade.

films.title: Send Help
films.note: Last week's No. 1, holding on: the Rachel McAdams/Dylan O'Brien horror that Sean pegs as a top-25 movie of the year and part of the case that January was actually great.

films.title: Melania
films.note: No. 3 at the box office; $7M, a lot for a doc, though on a $40M budget plus $35M in marketing. Sean argues it isn't really a documentary aesthetically: it's IP with a star at the center. Neither host has seen it and both hope the listener's-choice vote spares them.

films.title: The Intern
films.note: Cited in the Nancy Meyers time-travel update: Warner Bros. greenlit her first feature since this one (Anne Hathaway/Robert De Niro, $194M worldwide), dated Christmas 2027 with Penélope Cruz, Kieran Culkin, Jude Law, Emma Mackey and Owen Wilson. Sean won't believe it until the trucks show up.

films.title: Father of the Bride
films.note: Meyers's Steve Martin comedy, which also featured a young Kieran Culkin; she introduced an anniversary screening in L.A. and revealed the new project there. Confirmed to have screened at the PTA-curated wedding marathon.

segments.heading: The Catherine O’Hara Hall of Fame
segments.note: a tribute after her death at 71

films.title: Beetlejuice
films.note: Foundational for both hosts. The Day-O dinner-table scene is one of her most memorable moments.

films.title: Home Alone
films.note: She IS Kevin: the mother whose panicked 'Kevin!' is burned into a generation. Per Sean, she's not funny here at all, which is its own kind of range.

films.title: Best in Show
films.note: Amanda's demand for the list. 'God Loves a Terrier' is, she says, the hardest she has ever laughed in a film.

films.title: Waiting for Guffman
films.note: Her and Fred Willard in the Christopher Guest troupe, one of the collaborations that defined the back half of her career.

films.title: A Mighty Wind
films.note: Another Guest film; she and Eugene Levy sang the nominated song beautifully at the Oscars.

films.title: For Your Consideration
films.note: The Guest ensemble again: proof of how thoroughly the mockumentary auteur relied on her.

films.title: The Nightmare Before Christmas
films.note: The voice of Sally, one more foundational credit hiding in plain sight.

films.title: After Hours
films.note: Only about ten minutes of Scorsese, but hilarious: she torments Griffin Dunne with a phone number he's trying to remember.

films.title: Heartburn
films.note: Part of the run of auteurs (here Mike Nichols) who got interested in what she could bring.

films.title: Dick Tracy
films.note: Warren Beatty's comic-strip spectacle, another entry in her stealth-prolific studio run.

films.title: The Paper
films.note: Ron Howard's newsroom comedy, Sean's example of how many filmmakers wanted her.

films.title: Wyatt Earp
films.note: Lawrence Kasdan's Western; further range in the dramatic direction.

films.title: A Simple Twist of Fate
films.note: A slightly forgotten Steve Martin drama in which she plays the surrogate-mother best friend; Sean's deep-cut shout-out.

films.title: Orange County
films.note: Very funny here too, and tied to the recent John Candy documentary, given her long friendship with Candy going back to Canada.

films.title: Where the Wild Things Are
films.note: A voice performance for Spike Jonze, the kind of one-of-one casting everyone kept reaching for.

films.title: Beetlejuice Beetlejuice
films.note: The late-career return to the role, and one of the last times the hosts had cause to talk about her before the news.

segments.heading: Sean's Sundance
segments.note: the last Park City, watched virtually
segments.subject: Sundance 2026

films.title: Josephine
films.note: The one great movie Sean saw, and it wasn't close. Beth de Araujo directs Channing Tatum, Gemma Chan and an 8-year-old debut from Mason Reeves as a girl who witnesses a sexual assault on a morning walk and lives through the aftermath. Won both the Grand Jury Prize and the Audience Award (drama), a CODA/Minari/Whiplash/Precious double that usually points to Best Picture. Purposefully manipulative and, Sean says, shattering; no distributor yet, headed to Berlin. Compared to 'Adolescence' as a filmmaking-tool showcase.

films.title: The Best Summer
films.note: The most fun Sean had. Tamra Davis's shapeless, plotless home-movie doc of the 1995-96 Australian tour that put Beastie Boys, Pavement, Bikini Kill, Sonic Youth and Foo Fighters together: Kim Gordon on a couch, Sonic Youth doing 'Bull in the Heather.' 'Sometimes we just want to see the things that we love.'

films.title: Carousel
films.note: Chris Pine and Jenny Slate in a thoughtful midlife movie about being 43 and wondering what happened to your life. Sean flagged it the prior week; strong performances.

films.title: Nuisance Bear
films.note: Sean's best doc of the festival: polar bears in Manitoba, a classical nature film woven with an Inuit perspective, produced by A24. Some tough trap-and-relocate material.

films.title: Union County
films.note: A traditional Sundance drama with a great Will Poulter turn as a young man in a court-mandated Ohio drug program; effective for casting real people from the program.

films.title: Seized
films.note: The journalism doc, about the raid on the Marion County Record in Kansas: starts as First Amendment sermon, pivots to zippy small-town gossip. 'All news is local news.'

films.title: Ha-Chan, Shake Your Booty!
films.note: The most crowd-pleasing thing Sean saw: Rinko Kikuchi as a ballroom dancer who loses a partner and finds love, heavily indebted to Dirty Dancing.

films.title: Paralyzed by Hope: The Maria Bamford Story
films.note: The Maria Bamford documentary, fairly standard for the comedian-struggles genre but a good one; she lives in Altadena, and the fires figure near the end.

films.title: Night Nurse
films.note: The most fucked-up movie Sean saw. Georgia Bernstein directs a genuinely odd psychosexual codependency between a young night nurse and an elderly resident of the care facility: prank calls, orgies, trouble. Not scary, not sexy, just upsetting.

films.title: The Incomer
films.note: A quirky Scottish comedy by Louis Paxton: Domhnall Gleeson comes to remove two kooky siblings from a tiny island; Sean was pleased to learn Gayle Rankin is Scottish.

films.title: Bedford Park
films.note: Flawed but very good, already picked up by Sony Pictures Classics. Stephanie Ahn directs two Korean American strangers (Moon Choi, Son Seok-su) who half-fall for each other; a bit long, terrific performances.

films.title: People We Meet on Vacation
films.note: Brett Haley adapts the Emily Henry bestseller: Emily Bader and Tom Blyth as friends who take one vacation together a year for a decade. Amanda was wrong-footed (she expected an actual vacation movie) and thrown by a failed When Harry Met Sally homage; both agree it's Sparks-adjacent 'book-to-movie' fan service, not a real rom-com, and it isn't funny. Bader is a revelation (a Paula Abdul dance number included); Blyth, per Sean, a zero. Likely the most-watched movie of 2026 so far, and the hosts want more money spent, more locations, more going outside.

segments.heading: ‘The Moment’
segments.note: the Charli XCX mockumentary, in four theaters

films.title: The Moment
films.note: Aidan Zamiri directs, from an idea by Charli XCX (who also stars): a fake documentary about the Brat rollout and mounting the tour, with Rosanna Arquette, Kate Berlant, Jamie Demetriou, Alexander Skarsgård as a Sam Wrench-coded concert-film director and Kylie Jenner surprisingly good. Amanda loved it, calling it the most Amanda movie ever made; Sean found it far more accomplished than expected, closer to In the Loop or The Trip than Spinal Tap, and a savage two-hour Taylor Swift subtweet about what corporate success does to a punk-adjacent pop star.

films.title: Marty Supreme
films.note: The reason for the visit: Fisk's Oscar-nominated work rebuilding 1950s Lower East Side New York for Josh Safdie. He and decorator Adam Willis and DP Darius Khondji chased a documentary feel over a 'period film' look: a modular street-front system walked onto Orchard Street overnight, roped-and-aged paint, Yiddish signage, a bowling alley three hours out (pre-automatic-pinsetter), a built gas station. The one time he told Safdie no: painting Marty's room white for a Kubrick reference.

films.title: Badlands
films.note: Terrence Malick's debut, which changed Fisk's life, made him want to do film forever, and introduced him to Sissy Spacek: 55 years together.

films.title: Days of Heaven
films.note: Shot in Canada with no film industry to draw on: six carpenters brought from L.A., everything built from 2x4s and plywood, and the best fire he's ever seen, wheat cut in 50-foot rows and doused in diesel.

films.title: The New World
films.note: Where he met Emmanuel Lubezki, who later connected him to Iñárritu; he wanted to make it as a Pocahontas documentary but couldn't.

films.title: The Straight Story
films.note: A David Lynch family affair: he cast Spacek; Fisk and Lynch went to high school together and moved to California together.

films.title: Mulholland Drive
films.note: Built as an ABC TV pilot ABC rejected, then rebuilt for Canal+ as the feature now voted among the best films of its era. Fisk's proof that a film doesn't have to make money to be great.

films.title: The Revenant
films.note: He prayed to 'the movie God' just to see the fort finished as the production nearly shut down; Iñárritu got the money and it never did.

films.title: There Will Be Blood
films.note: One of two Paul Thomas Anderson films: practical black smoke made by burning diesel fuel in pans, the kind of thing you can't do anymore.

films.title: Raggedy Man
films.note: His first film as director; he made a few, then decided he'd rather build worlds than deal with studios.

films.title: Violets Are Blue
films.note: His own directorial work, which Sean had just seen for the first time and loved: non-judgmental, a tone movies don't have anymore.

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: His answer to the closing question: a world he'd never otherwise see, and the reason he loves film.

films.title: Anna Karenina
films.note: Joe Wright and Sarah Greenwood's no-money, one-theater design that blew his mind. Wright, he says, is one of our great under-appreciated filmmakers.
